figures.caption: Figure 1: Illustration of Cartesian pendulum systems and their one-dimensional latent representations induced by our method. The dynamical system takes a context variable (e.g., initial conditions or external disturbances) as input and generates the corresponding pendulum trajectory. A system is considered stable when the context follows the “safe” distribution. Representative trajectories under three exogenous context distributions are shown top: green segments satisfy the angular stability criterion, while red segments indicate violations. Under stable regimes, the learned latent representation is approximately uniform; under unstable regimes, it deviates noticeably from uniformity.
Table 2: Summary of the synthetic shift settings.

Figure 3: Comparison between L2T and other baselines under various distribution shifts. Green and red regions denote stable and unstable contexts, respectively, while dashed gray and solid black contours represent the baseline and shifted distributions. The top row of each panel shows baseline (P0) and shifted (P1) context distributions in the 2D space across representative regimes: null (H0 → H0), boundary transition (H0 → H1), and alternative (H1 → H1). The bottom row presents empirical rejection rates of L2T and baseline methods, for varying shift magnitudes. The dotted horizontal line marks the nominal level α. The L2T achieves reliable type-I error control while maintaining better detection power near critical stability boundaries.

Figure 4: Trajectory visualization for generator G01. Blue curves denote stable trajectories, while red curves denote unstable ones. Each subplot corresponds to a different system variable: active power, excitation current, rotor angle, rotor speed, and terminal voltage. Stable trajectories exhibit consistent and structured dynamics, whereas unstable trajectories show greater variability and dispersion.
Table 1: Comparison of empirical Type I and Type II errors at significance level 0.1.

Figure 2: Overview of the proposed “Learning-to-Test” (L2T) paradigm. Training phase (bottom): Baseline contextsXi ∼ P0 and their DAE-governed trajectories Si are used to jointly train an encoder ϕ, dynamical update φ, and decoder ψ. The objectives combine trajectory reconstruction (Lrec) and latent uniformity regularization (Lreg), yielding a stability-aware latent representation Ui ∼ Q0. Testing phase (top): For new contexts Xj ∼ P1, the encoder produces latent variables Uj ∼ Q1, and instability detection is reduced to a distributional test between Q1 and Q0 in latent space.
